    {"repo": "openjdk/jdk", "path": "src/java.base/share/classes/java/security/Provider.java", "symbol": "putService"}
  ],
  "text": "The OpenJDK security provider registration framework is implemented across multiple Java source files that work together to provide a pluggable architecture for cryptographic and security algorithms.\n\n## Base Provider Class\nThe foundation is **java.security.Provider** (src/java.base/share/classes/java/security/Provider.java), which serves as the abstract base class for all security providers. It defines the core infrastructure including the **putService()** method (line 1221) that providers use to register algorithmic services, and the inner **Service** class (line 1500) that encapsulates service metadata.\n\n## Provider Implementations\nMultiple concrete provider implementations extend Provider and register their algorithms:\n\n1. **sun.security.provider.Sun** (src/java.base/share/classes/sun/security/provider/Sun.java) - Provides core cryptographic algorithms including DSA key/parameter generation, SHA-1 and MD5 digests, SecureRandom, X.509 certificates, PKIX certification path validation, PKCS12/JKS keystores, and Java Policy configuration. It delegates service registration to **SunEntries** helper class.\n\n2. **sun.security.rsa.SunRsaSign** (src/java.base/share/classes/sun/security/rsa/SunRsaSign.java) - Provides RSA signatures, RSA key factory, and RSA key pair generation capabilities. Uses **SunRsaSignEntries** helper for service registration.\n\n3. **com.sun.crypto.provider.SunJCE** (src/java.base/share/classes/com/sun/crypto/provider/SunJCE.java) - The primary cryptographic provider implementing RSA encryption, DES, Triple DES, AES, Blowfish, ARCFOUR, RC2, PBE (password-based encryption), Diffie-Hellman key agreement, HMAC variants, JCEKS keystore, DHKEM, and ML-KEM. It uses putService() calls with Provider.Service objects to register each algorithm.\n\n4. **sun.security.ssl.SunJSSE** (src/java.base/share/classes/sun/security/ssl/SunJSSE.java) - Provides SSL/TLS infrastructure including KeyManagerFactory (SunX509/PKIX implementations), TrustManagerFactory, SSLContext (supporting TLSv1.0 through TLSv1.3 and DTLS variants), and PKCS12 keystore for SSL/TLS communications.\n\n5. **sun.security.ec.SunEC** (src/java.base/share/classes/sun/security/ec/SunEC.java) - Implements elliptic curve cryptography including EdDSA signatures (Ed25519/Ed448), ECDSA signatures with various SHA algorithms, EC key factories and key pair generators, and EC parameter specifications.\n\n6. **apple.security.AppleProvider** (src/java.base/macosx/classes/apple/security/AppleProvider.java) - Platform-specific provider for macOS integration.\n\n## Lazy-Loading Registration Mechanism\nThe provider registration framework uses lazy-loading to defer instantiation of providers until needed:\n\n**sun.security.jca.ProviderConfig** (src/java.base/share/classes/sun/security/jca/ProviderConfig.java) is the central lazy-loading mechanism. It encapsulates provider configuration (name and optional arguments) and implements the **getProvider()** method (line 152) using double-checked locking pattern. The method maintains a mapping of provider names to their concrete classes (lines 168-215), supporting both built-in providers (SUN, SunRsaSign, SunJCE, SunJSSE, SunEC) and dynamically loaded providers through ServiceLoader and reflection-based legacy loading. ProviderConfig tracks loading attempts and prevents infinite recursion during provider initialization.\n\n## Provider Collection Management\n**sun.security.jca.ProviderList** (src/java.base/share/classes/sun/security/jca/ProviderList.java) maintains an immutable list of ProviderConfig objects representing the system-wide provider preferences. It loads provider configuration from security properties in the java.security file via the **fromSecurityProperties()** static method (line 88) and provides operations to add, remove, and insert providers while maintaining immutability.\n\n**sun.security.jca.Providers** (src/java.base/share/classes/sun/security/jca/Providers.java) is the collection management class providing thread-local and system-wide provider list access. It initializes the default provider list during class loading (lines 51-56) and maintains special provider configurations for JAR verification.\n\n## Public API\n**java.security.Security** (src/java.base/share/classes/java/security/Security.java) is the public API that applications use to interact with the security provider framework through methods like getProviders() and getProvider(String name).\n\nThe architecture enables dynamic provider loading, algorithm pluggability, and separation between provider implementation and provider discovery, making the Java security framework extensible while maintaining backward compatibility."
}

conversation_events.text:
# OpenJDK Security Provider Registration Framework

## Your Task

Find all Java source files in openjdk/jdk that implement the security provider registration framework. Identify: the base Provider class with putService() (java.security.Provider), the SunJCE provider that registers crypto algorithms, the SunJSSE provider for SSL/TLS factories, the SunEC provider for elliptic curve algorithms, the Sun provider for core algorithms, and the ProviderConfig lazy-loading mechanism that maps provider names to classes.

## Context

You are working on a codebase task involving repos from the migration domain.

## Available Resources

The local `/workspace/` directory contains: sg-evals/jdk--742e735d.

**Note:** Additional repositories are accessible via Sourcegraph MCP tools:
- `sg-evals/jdk--742e735d` (openjdk/jdk)

## Output Format

Create a file at `/workspace/answer.json` with your findings in the following structure:

```json
{
  "files": [
    {"repo": "org/repo-name", "path": "relative/path/to/file.go"}
  ],
  "symbols": [
    {"repo": "org/repo-name", "path": "relative/path/to/file.go", "symbol": "SymbolName"}
  ],
  "chain": [
    {"repo": "org/repo-name", "path": "relative/path/to/file.go", "symbol": "FunctionName"}
  ],
  "text": "Narrative explanation of your findings, citing repos and file paths."
}
```

Include only the fields relevant to this task. Your answer is evaluated against a closed-world oracle — completeness matters.

## Evaluation

Your answer will be scored on:
- **File recall and precision**: Did you find all relevant files?
I'll help you find the Java source files implementing the security provider registration framework in openjdk/jdk. Let me start by exploring the workspace structure.
Let me search for the Provider.Service class which is used to register services:

Perfect! I've successfully completed the task. I've identified and documented all the key Java source files that implement the OpenJDK security provider registration framework.

## Summary of Findings

I created `/workspace/answer.json` with comprehensive information about the security provider registration framework, including:

### **Core Files Identified:**

1. **Base Framework:**
   - `java.security.Provider` - Base class with `putService()` method
   - `java.security.Security` - Public API

2. **Provider Implementations:**
   - `sun.security.provider.Sun` - Core algorithms (DSA, SHA-1, MD5, X.509, etc.)
   - `com.sun.crypto.provider.SunJCE` - Cryptographic algorithms (AES, DES, RSA, HMAC, etc.)
   - `sun.security.ssl.SunJSSE` - SSL/TLS factories and KeyManagers
   - `sun.security.ec.SunEC` - Elliptic curve cryptography (EdDSA, ECDSA)
   - `sun.security.rsa.SunRsaSign` - RSA signatures

3. **Lazy-Loading Mechanism:**
   - `sun.security.jca.ProviderConfig` - Central lazy-loader with `getProvider()` method
   - Uses double-checked locking and maintains provider name-to-class mappings
   - Supports both built-in providers and dynamic loading via ServiceLoader

4. **Provider Management:**
   - `sun.security.jca.ProviderList` - Immutable list of provider configurations
   - `sun.security.jca.Providers` - Collection management with thread-local support

The answer includes detailed narrative documentation explaining how the framework enables dynamic provider loading and algorithm pluggability while maintaining backward compatibility.

It defines the core infrastructure including the **putService()** method (line 1221) that providers use to register algorithmic services, and the inner **Service** class (line 1500) that encapsulates service metadata.\n\n## Provider Implementations\nMultiple concrete provider implementations extend Provider and register their algorithms:\n\n1. **sun.security.provider.Sun** (src/java.base/share/classes/sun/security/provider/Sun.java) - Provides core cryptographic algorithms including DSA key/parameter generation, SHA-1 and MD5 digests, SecureRandom, X.509 certificates, PKIX certification path validation, PKCS12/JKS keystores, and Java Policy configuration. It delegates service registration to **SunEntries** helper class.\n\n2. **sun.security.rsa.SunRsaSign** (src/java.base/share/classes/sun/security/rsa/SunRsaSign.java) - Provides RSA signatures, RSA key factory, and RSA key pair generation capabilities. It uses putService() calls with Provider.Service objects to register each algorithm.\n\n4. **sun.security.ssl.SunJSSE** (src/java.base/share/classes/sun/security/ssl/SunJSSE.java) - Provides SSL/TLS infrastructure including KeyManagerFactory (SunX509/PKIX implementations), TrustManagerFactory, SSLContext (supporting TLSv1.0 through TLSv1.3 and DTLS variants), and PKCS12 keystore for SSL/TLS communications.\n\n5. **sun.security.ec.SunEC** (src/java.base/share/classes/sun/security/ec/SunEC.java) - Implements elliptic curve cryptography including EdDSA signatures (Ed25519/Ed448), ECDSA signatures with various SHA algorithms, EC key factories and key pair generators, and EC parameter specifications.\n\n6. **apple.security.AppleProvider** (src/java.base/macosx/classes/apple/security/AppleProvider.java) - Platform-specific provider for macOS integration.\n\n## Lazy-Loading Registration Mechanism\nThe provider registration framework uses lazy-loading to defer instantiation of providers until needed:\n\n**sun.security.jca.ProviderConfig** (src/java.base/share/classes/sun/security/jca/ProviderConfig.java) is the central lazy-loading mechanism.
